passages.explanation: Jesus is praying in a certain place. When he finishes, one of his disciples says: Lord, teach us to pray, just as John taught his disciples. He says: when you pray, say — Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come. Give us each day our daily bread. Forgive us our sins, for we also forgive everyone who sins against us. And lead us not into temptation. Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer is shorter than Matthew's. It covers worship, God's kingdom, daily provision, forgiveness, and protection. It's a framework, not a formula — every essential category of prayer in five lines.

Jesus tells a parable: suppose you go to a friend at midnight and ask for three loaves because a visitor has arrived and you have nothing to offer. The friend says: don't bother me, the door is locked, my children and I are in bed. Jesus says: even if he won't get up because of friendship, he'll get up because of your shameless audacity and give you as much as you need. So ask and it will be given, seek and you will find, knock and the door will be opened. For everyone who asks receives, the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks the door will be opened. Which father, if his son asks for a fish, will give him a snake? Or if he asks for an egg, will give him a scorpion? If you then, though you are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him! The point isn't that God is reluctant and must be pestered — it's that if even a grumpy neighbour will respond to persistence, how much more will a loving Father. Luke's version ends with the Holy Spirit, not just 'good things' as in Matthew. The best gift is God himself.

Jesus drives out a demon that is mute. When the demon leaves, the man speaks. The crowd is amazed, but some say: he drives out demons by Beelzebul, the prince of demons. Others test him by asking for a sign from heaven. Jesus knows their thoughts and responds: any kingdom divided against itself will be ruined. If Satan is divided against himself, how can his kingdom stand? If I drive out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your followers drive them out? But if I drive out demons by the finger of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you. When a strong man, fully armed, guards his house, his possessions are safe. But when someone stronger attacks and overpowers him, he takes away the armour he relied on and divides up his plunder. Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters. When an impure spirit comes out of a person, it goes through arid places seeking rest. Finding none, it says: I'll go back to my house. It finds the house swept clean and put in order. Then it goes and brings seven other spirits more wicked than itself, and they move in. The final condition of that person is worse than the first. Jesus isn't just driving out demons — he's invading enemy territory. And empty reformation without being filled with something new is dangerous.

A woman in the crowd calls out: blessed is the mother who gave you birth and nursed you! Jesus replies: blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and obey it. Jesus redirects veneration of his mother toward something more fundamental. Biology doesn't confer blessing — obedience does.

As the crowds increase, Jesus says: this is a wicked generation. It asks for a sign, but none will be given except the sign of Jonah. For as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so also will the Son of Man be to this generation. The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment and condemn this generation, because she came from the ends of the earth to listen to Solomon's wisdom — and something greater than Solomon is here. The men of Nineveh will stand at the judgment and condemn this generation, because they repented at the preaching of Jonah — and something greater than Jonah is here. No one lights a lamp and puts it in a place where it will be hidden. Instead they put it on its stand so those who come in can see the light. Your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eyes are healthy, your whole body is full of light. When they're unhealthy, your body is full of darkness. See to it that the light within you is not darkness. The sign-seekers already have all the evidence they need — the problem is their eyes, not the light.

A Pharisee invites Jesus to eat with him. Jesus goes in and reclines at the table without first washing — the Pharisee is surprised. Jesus responds with a series of woes. To the Pharisees: you clean the outside of the cup and dish, but inside you're full of greed and wickedness. You give God a tenth of your mint, rue, and every garden herb, but you neglect justice and the love of God. You love the most important seats in the synagogues and respectful greetings in the marketplaces. You are like unmarked graves that people walk over without knowing. To the experts in the law: you load people with burdens they can hardly carry, and you won't lift one finger to help them. You build tombs for the prophets your ancestors killed. You've taken away the key to knowledge — you didn't enter yourselves, and you hindered those who were entering. The Pharisees and teachers of the law begin to oppose him fiercely, trying to catch him in something he might say. Jesus doesn't criticise their devotion — he criticises the mismatch between external performance and internal reality. The system is producing hypocrisy, not holiness.
